schema.headline: What Is a Crypto Token? Understanding Types and Functionality
schema.alternativeHeadline: Understanding Crypto Tokens: Types, Standards, and How They Work
schema.description: A crypto token is a digital asset built on existing blockchains. Learn token vs coin differences, types, and standards.

image.caption: Diagram showing different types of crypto tokens built on blockchain infrastructure

itemListElement.name: What Is a Crypto Token? Understanding Types and Functionality

schema.name: Types of Crypto Tokens
schema.description: Comprehensive overview of different crypto token categories and their characteristics

itemListElement.name: Utility Tokens
itemListElement.description: Tokens that provide access to products or services within specific ecosystems

itemListElement.name: Security Tokens
itemListElement.description: Tokens representing ownership rights, profit-sharing, or investment contracts

itemListElement.name: Stablecoins
itemListElement.description: Tokens designed to maintain stable value relative to fiat currencies

itemListElement.name: Governance Tokens
itemListElement.description: Tokens granting holders voting rights in decentralised protocols

itemListElement.name: NFTs
itemListElement.description: Non-fungible tokens representing unique digital assets

itemListElement.name: Wrapped Tokens
itemListElement.description: Tokens representing assets from one blockchain on another blockchain

schema.name: Understanding Crypto Token Types and Their Banking Requirements
schema.description: Guide to identifying different crypto token types and their operational requirements

step.name: Identify token vs coin
step.text: Determine if the asset is a native blockchain asset (coin) or built on existing infrastructure (token)

step.name: Classify token type
step.text: Categorise the token as utility, security, stablecoin, governance, NFT, or wrapped token

step.name: Check token standards
step.text: Verify the token standard (ERC-20, ERC-721, BEP-20, etc.) to understand technical requirements

step.name: Assess banking requirements
step.text: Evaluate fiat banking needs based on token type and operational patterns

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a crypto token and a coin?

acceptedAnswer.text: A coin is the native digital asset of a blockchain (like Bitcoin on Bitcoin or Ether on Ethereum), whilst a token is built on top of an existing blockchain using smart contracts. Coins secure their own networks; tokens leverage existing blockchain infrastructure.

mainEntity.name: What are the main types of crypto tokens?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main types are utility tokens (access to services), security tokens (represent ownership rights), stablecoins (pegged to fiat currencies), governance tokens (voting rights in protocols), NFTs (unique digital assets), and wrapped tokens (representations of assets from other blockchains).

ERC-20 is the most common token standard on Ethereum, defining how fungible tokens should behave. It specifies functions like transfer, balance checking, and approval mechanisms. Most tokens on Ethereum, including USDC and UNI, follow this standard.

mainEntity.name: How do stablecoins maintain their value?

acceptedAnswer.text: Stablecoins maintain their value through different mechanisms: fiat-collateralised stablecoins like USDC hold equivalent fiat reserves in banks, crypto-collateralised ones use overcollateralisation with crypto assets, and algorithmic stablecoins use market mechanisms to maintain their peg.

mainEntity.name: Why do crypto platforms need fiat banking for token operations?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crypto platforms need fiat banking for on/off-ramps (converting fiat to tokens and vice versa), reserve management for stablecoin issuers, settlement between platforms, and compliance with regulatory requirements for certain token types like security tokens.

mainEntity.name: What's the best banking infrastructure for crypto token platforms?

acceptedAnswer.text: Fiat Republic provides multi-bank API access, 24/7 instant settlement through EagleNet, and crypto-native compliance through Oxygen, enabling crypto platforms to handle the fiat side of token operations without debanking risk.
